message:
fix(storage): escalate post-commit blocked-cache refresh errors when foreign keys are disabled

The post-commit blocked-cache refresh had a subtle severity bug. When
`refresh_blocked_cache_after_commit` returned an error, the outer
call site always logged it at warn and swallowed the `Result`. That's
the right behavior when the transaction's own `finish_foreign_key_
suppressed_result` has already re-enabled FK enforcement — the cache
is just stale, future reads will see the freshness marker and
recompute. But when FK enforcement is still OFF (rare, only reachable
if the refresh itself failed *between* `foreign_keys = OFF` and
`foreign_keys = ON`), swallowing the error hides an integrity-class
issue: subsequent writes on this connection would silently bypass FK
constraints.

Introduce `handle_blocked_cache_refresh_error(&self, op, error)` that
classifies the outcome:
- FK enforcement is ON: keep the existing behaviour. Warn with
  operation + error context and return Ok — the cache is marked
  stale and will recompute on demand.
- FK enforcement is OFF: escalate to `BeadsError::WithContext` with a
  message that names both the failed op and the fact that FK
  enforcement is disabled, boxing the original error. The caller
  must now observe the failure and either re-enable enforcement or
  abort.
- FK status could not be queried: also escalate to WithContext, this
  time including the PRAGMA-query error in the context prefix so
  debugging can start from the right failure.

Call site in the post-commit refresh block switches from the ad-hoc
warn! to `?`-propagating through the new helper.

Regression test `test_blocked_cache_refresh_restores_foreign_keys_after_rebuild`
opens an in-memory storage, turns FK enforcement OFF, then exercises
a Full refresh and asserts (a) it succeeds and (b) FK enforcement is
ON afterwards. Locks the "refresh must leave FKs enabled" invariant
so future reorganizations can't silently regress the exit-state
contract.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
